text.title: Token Taxonomy and Naming Convention
text.summary: Three-layer token taxonomy and naming convention rules that keep design systems lean and maintainable.

rule.statement: Use exactly three layers — primitives, semantic, component — never four or more.
rule.rationale: A fourth layer (e.g., sub-component variants) creates indirection depth that makes runtime debugging and visual diff verification impractical.

rule.statement: Add component tokens only when a single component needs a documented exception to a semantic token; justify every component token in its `$description`.
rule.rationale: Unjustified component tokens frequently duplicate semantic tokens and double the rename surface area on every rebrand.

rule.statement: Name tokens using `{category}.{property}.{variant}.{state}` dot-path notation; describe purpose, never appearance.
rule.rationale: Appearance-based names (`blue-500`) encode the current design decision into every consumer; purpose-based names (`color.action.primary`) survive rebranding without consumer changes.

rule.statement: Limit dot-path depth to 4 segments maximum (`category.property.variant.state`).
rule.rationale: Deeper paths indicate a missing taxonomy layer or Cartesian explosion of variants; LLMs generate full state-variant grids nobody uses if not bounded.

section.title: Lean-First Principle

rule.statement: Add a new token only when no existing semantic token covers the case; run a duplicate-value detector in CI.
rule.rationale: Tokens with identical values under different names are a smell: either the existing token should be reused, or one of them is redundant.

rule.statement: Add a `$description` to every semantic token explaining why it exists, not what value it holds.
rule.rationale: Future maintainers and LLMs read descriptions to decide whether to reuse or create a token; missing descriptions produce duplication.

section.reference: Linter that flags names not matching convention and tokens with duplicate values.
